panel.origin: Scored when the puck fully crosses the goal line into the net. The fundamental unit of scoring — most games are decided by just a few.
panel.mixology: He snapped a wrist shot top corner for the game-winning goal with seconds left.
panel.examples: From the old sense of 'goal' as a boundary or finish line. In hockey the puck must wholly cross the line between the posts and under the bar.

panel.origin: Credit given to the one or two players who passed or set up a goal-scorer. Assists count as points alongside goals in a player's stats.
panel.mixology: She threaded a perfect pass for the assist, setting up her linemate's easy tap-in.
panel.examples: From 'assist' (to help). Hockey uniquely rewards up to two helpers per goal, valuing playmaking nearly as much as finishing.

panel.origin: Scoring three goals in a single game by one player. A celebrated feat, traditionally honored by fans throwing hats onto the ice.
panel.mixology: After his third goal, hats rained down from the stands to salute the hat trick.
panel.examples: Borrowed from cricket, where a bowler taking three wickets earned a new hat. The hat-throwing tradition cemented the term in hockey lore.

panel.origin: When a team has more skaters than the opponent because of a penalty, giving them a temporary scoring advantage. A pivotal momentum swing.
panel.mixology: Up a man on the power play, they cycled the puck until a lane opened for the shot.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the team 'plays' with a 'power' advantage in numbers. Converting power plays is often the difference between winning and losing.

panel.origin: When a team is short-handed due to a penalty and must defend without the puck advantage, trying to 'kill' the time until full strength returns.
panel.mixology: Their penalty kill held firm, blocking shots and clearing the puck until the penalty expired.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the short-handed team works to 'kill off' the penalty time without conceding. A great PK can swing momentum back to the defending side.

panel.origin: When an attacker gets behind the defense with a clear path to the goalie and no defenders to beat. A thrilling one-on-one scoring chance.
panel.mixology: He sprang a breakaway, racing in alone and deking the goalie for a highlight-reel goal.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the player 'breaks away' free of the defense. The pure one-on-one duel with the goalie is one of hockey's most exciting moments.

panel.origin: Shooting the puck immediately upon receiving a pass, without stopping it first. A quick, powerful play that catches goalies out of position.
panel.mixology: She blasted a one-timer off the cross-ice feed before the goalie could slide over.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the puck is struck in 'one' motion the moment it arrives. Timing it perfectly produces one of the hardest, fastest shots in the game.

panel.origin: A goal scored into an open net after a trailing team has pulled its goalie for an extra attacker. The clinching goal that seals a win.
panel.mixology: With the goalie pulled, she fired the puck the length of the ice for an empty-netter.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the net is 'empty' of a goalie. A team losing late pulls its goalie to attack, risking exactly this kind of game-sealing goal.

panel.origin: The central forward who plays the middle of the ice, takes face-offs, and links offense and defense. Often a team's playmaking hub.
panel.mixology: As center, he won the face-off cleanly and immediately set the offense in motion.
panel.examples: Descriptive: this forward patrols the 'center' of the ice. Centers are typically the most all-around players, strong at both ends and in the dot.

panel.origin: A forward who plays along the sides ('wings') of the rink, supporting the center. Left and right wingers focus on scoring and forechecking.
panel.mixology: The winger hugged the boards, waiting for the breakout pass to spring up the wing.
panel.examples: From the 'wings' (sides) of the playing area, like wings of a building or bird. Wingers patrol the outer lanes, finishing plays the center creates.

panel.origin: A player whose primary job is stopping the opposing offense and protecting their own goal, while also starting attacks from the back.
panel.mixology: The defenseman stepped up at the blue line to break up the rush before it started.
panel.examples: Descriptive: this player's role is 'defense'. Working in pairs, they're the last line before the goalie and increasingly contribute to offense too.

panel.origin: Goaltender — the heavily padded player who guards the net and stops shots. The last line of defense and often a game's most important player.
panel.mixology: The goalie sprawled across the crease to rob the shooter with a glove save.
panel.examples: From 'goal' + 'tender' (one who tends or guards). The goaltender's unique equipment and role make them unlike any other player on the ice.

panel.origin: A tough, physical player whose role is to protect teammates, intimidate opponents, and respond to rough play — sometimes by fighting.
panel.mixology: The enforcer answered the cheap shot on the star, dropping the gloves to defend his teammate.
panel.examples: From 'enforce' — they 'enforce' an unwritten code of respect and retaliation. A traditional, now-fading role unique to hockey's physical culture.

panel.origin: A unit of three forwards (and often a defensive pairing) who play together as a coordinated group, rotating on and off the ice in shifts.
panel.mixology: The top line clicked all night, the three forwards reading each other perfectly.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the forwards line up and play together as a set. Teams roll several lines through short, high-intensity shifts to stay fresh.

panel.origin: The team leader, identified by a 'C' on the jersey, who represents the players, communicates with officials, and sets the tone on and off the ice.
panel.mixology: The captain rallied the bench during the intermission, demanding a stronger third period.
panel.examples: From the military/nautical sense of a leader. Hockey formalizes it with the 'C' worn on the jersey; assistant captains wear an 'A'.

panel.origin: A violation called when an attacking player crosses into the offensive zone ahead of the puck. Stops the rush and forces a face-off.
panel.mixology: The linesman waved off the goal because the winger was offside, entering the zone too early.
panel.examples: From the broad sports concept of being on the wrong 'side' of the play. In hockey, players can't precede the puck over the blue line into the attacking zone.

panel.origin: A violation called when a player shoots the puck from behind center ice all the way across the opponent's goal line untouched. Stops play to prevent stalling.
panel.mixology: Under pressure, the defender flung the puck down the ice, drawing an icing call.
panel.examples: From the old sense of shooting the puck the length of the 'ice'. The rule prevents teams from endlessly clearing the puck to waste time.

panel.origin: A punishment for breaking the rules, sending the offender to the penalty box and leaving their team short-handed for two or more minutes.
panel.mixology: His trip drew a two-minute penalty, putting his team down a skater.
panel.examples: From 'penalty' (a punishment). Hockey's penalties remove the offender from play, directly creating the power-play and penalty-kill situations.

panel.origin: A penalty for hitting an opponent with the stick carried above shoulder or waist height. A common, dangerous infraction.
panel.mixology: She caught an edge of the stick to the face, and the ref signaled high-sticking.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the stick is carried illegally 'high'. The rule protects players from the serious facial and head injuries an errant high stick can cause.

panel.origin: The method of starting or restarting play, where an official drops the puck between two opposing players who battle to win possession.
panel.mixology: He crouched low at the face-off, winning the draw cleanly back to his defenseman.
panel.examples: Descriptive: two players 'face off' against each other. Every period and stoppage restarts with a face-off, making the skill of winning draws valuable.

panel.origin: Using the body or stick to legally separate an opponent from the puck. 'Body checking' and 'poke checking' are core defensive skills.
panel.mixology: He laid a clean body check along the boards, knocking the puck carrier off the play.
panel.examples: From 'check' (to stop or restrain). Legal checking is fundamental to hockey's physical defense, though illegal checks (from behind, to the head) draw penalties.

panel.origin: When an official signals a penalty but lets play continue until the offending team touches the puck — letting the other team attack with an extra skater first.
panel.mixology: Seeing the delayed penalty signal, they pulled the goalie for a six-on-five attack.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the penalty is 'delayed' so the non-offending team isn't disadvantaged by an immediate whistle, letting them press their momentary advantage.

panel.origin: Either of the two blue lines dividing the rink into zones. Crossing them governs offside and marks the boundary of the offensive and defensive zones.
panel.mixology: He held the puck at the blue line, waiting for his teammates to clear the zone before entering.
panel.examples: Named simply for its color on the ice. The blue lines define the three zones and are central to the offside rule that shapes how attacks develop.

panel.origin: The marked area directly in front of the goal, reserved for the goalie. Attackers interfere with the goalie here at their peril.
panel.mixology: The goal was disallowed because an attacker was standing in the crease, interfering with the goalie.
panel.examples: From the sense of a marked line or fold (a 'crease'). It's the goaltender's protected zone, and goals can be waved off for crease violations.

panel.origin: The central area of the rink between the two blue lines, belonging to neither team's offense or defense. The transition space between zones.
panel.mixology: They clogged the neutral zone with defenders, strangling the opponent's rush before it built speed.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the 'neutral' middle ground between attacking zones. Controlling it ('the neutral-zone trap') is a classic defensive strategy.

panel.origin: The wooden and glass walls surrounding the rink that keep the puck in play. Much of hockey's physical battle happens along the boards.
panel.mixology: The two wingers crashed into the boards, fighting for the loose puck in the corner.
panel.examples: From the literal wooden 'boards' (now reinforced with glass) ringing the ice. They keep play continuous and are a constant arena for physical contests.

panel.origin: The long implement each player uses to handle, pass, and shoot the puck. The goalie's stick has a distinct, wider blade.
panel.mixology: He flexed his stick into the ice, loading up the slap shot.
panel.examples: From the everyday 'stick'. The modern composite hockey stick, with its curved blade, is the essential tool for every skill in the game.

panel.origin: The hard, frozen rubber disc that players shoot into the net to score. Small, fast, and frozen before games to reduce bouncing.
panel.mixology: The puck rocketed off his stick at nearly a hundred miles an hour.
panel.examples: Possibly from the Irish 'poc' (to poke or strike) or the mischievous sprite Puck. The vulcanized rubber disc is frozen before play to keep it from bouncing.

panel.origin: The machine that resurfaces the ice between periods, shaving and smoothing it and laying down fresh water. A beloved fixture of the hockey experience.
panel.mixology: Fans watched the Zamboni circle the rink, leaving a gleaming sheet of fresh ice behind.
panel.examples: A brand name, from inventor Frank Zamboni, who built the first ice-resurfacing machine in 1949. Like 'Kleenex', the name became the generic term.

panel.origin: Slang for the puck. 'Biscuit in the basket' is a colorful way to describe scoring a goal.
panel.mixology: He put the biscuit in the basket on a beautiful backhand.
panel.examples: Affectionate hockey slang likening the round, flat puck to a biscuit. 'Biscuit in the basket' is among the sport's most cherished bits of color commentary.

panel.origin: The gap between a goalie's legs. Scoring 'through the five-hole' means slipping the puck between the pads.
panel.mixology: She read the goalie dropping early and slid it through the five-hole.
panel.examples: From the numbering of a goalie's save zones — the four corners are holes one through four, and the gap between the legs is the fifth 'hole'.

panel.origin: Slang for an assist. A player with lots of 'apples' is a gifted passer and playmaker.
panel.mixology: He didn't score but racked up three apples, setting up every goal.
panel.examples: Old hockey slang of uncertain origin for an assist — possibly rhyming or simply a colorful nickname that stuck in the sport's rich vocabulary.

panel.origin: Slang for the penalty box, where penalized players must sit and serve their time. A wry name for the box of shame.
panel.mixology: He slammed the door of the sin bin after his careless penalty.
panel.examples: A playful nickname casting the penalty box as a place to atone for one's on-ice 'sins' — a beloved bit of hockey humor.

panel.origin: A fake or feint used to trick a defender or goalie out of position. To 'deke' someone is to outwit them with a deceptive move.
panel.mixology: He deked left, then cut right, leaving the goalie sprawling the wrong way.
panel.examples: A clipping of 'decoy'. The art of the deke — selling a fake move to create an opening — is one of hockey's most exciting individual skills.

panel.origin: Slang for a goal celebration. A big 'celly' is an exuberant, theatrical reaction after scoring.
panel.mixology: His over-the-top celly — sliding across the ice on his knees — fired up the whole bench.
panel.examples: A casual shortening of 'celebration'. The creativity and exuberance of cellys have become a celebrated, personality-filled part of modern hockey culture.
